cards.answer: A broker stores partitions, serves reads and writes, and participates in replication. Capacity, partition placement, and broker count drive throughput, fault isolation, and recovery speed.
cards.prompt: What is a Kafka broker and why does it matter in a production cluster?
cards.title: Kafka Broker

cards.answer: A partition is consumed by at most one consumer in a group, so adding consumers increases parallelism only up to the partition count. Too few partitions caps throughput; too many increases coordination and storage overhead.
cards.prompt: How do consumer groups scale reads and what tradeoff do they impose?
cards.title: Consumer Groups

cards.answer: Use EOS when Kafka producers, brokers, and consumers all participate correctly in transactions. You still need idempotent downstream writes because external databases and side effects are outside Kafka's transaction boundary.
cards.prompt: When should you trust Kafka exactly-once semantics and when is idempotency still required?
cards.title: Exactly-once Semantics

subtitle: Build stronger event-streaming instincts
summary: A small sample deck that demonstrates the JSON format for importing or updating a deck.
title: Kafka Core
